coredata.dc:title: Implementing neural network approach to create carbon-based optical nanosensor of heavy metal ions in liquid media
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sarmanova, O.E.
Laptinskiy, K.A.
Burikov, S.A.
Chugreeva, G.N.
Dolenko, T.A.
coredata.dc:description: The present study is devoted to the creation of multifunctional optical carbon dots-based nanosensor to simultaneously measure concentrations of metal ions (Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+) and NO3 – anion in liquid media. Such nanosensor operates on the basis of its fluorescence (FL) change under the influence of ions in the medium. However, the absence of analytical model, describing CD FL mechanism, the superposition of various luminescence quenching mechanisms during the interaction of carbon dots (CD) with cations, hampers the usage of classical approaches to solve this inverse multiparametric spectroscopic problem. To solve it neural networks were used that analyzed complex fluorescence signal from CD aqueous suspensions comprising Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, NO3 – ions in the concentration range from 0 to 4.95 mM. The following neural network architectures ensured optical spectroscopy inverse problem solution: multilayer perceptrons, 1D and 2D convolutional neural networks. The developed sensor enables simultaneous determination of the concentrations of heavy metal ions Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+ with a root mean squared error of 0.28 mM, 0.79 mM, 0.24 mM respectively. Based on the data given in the literature we can assert that the accuracy of the studied nanosensor satisfies the needs of monitoring the composition of waste and technological water. The developed nanosensor has a unique multimodality: with the simplicity of the synthesis protocol the sensor enables simultaneous determination of three heavy metal ions concentrations, while analogues are being developed mainly to measure the concentration of one (in rare cases two) heavy metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metal ions
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Convolutional neural networks
Carbon dots

The contribution of O.E. Sarmanova (programming and training of neural networks) was supported by theFoundation for the Advancement of Theoretical Physics and Mathematics “BASIS” (Project No. 19-2-6-6-1).

The study was carried out at the expense of the Russian Science Foundation No. 22-12-00138 , https://rscf.ru/project /22-12-00138 / (T.A.Dolenko, S.A.Burikov, K.A.Laptinskiy – planning and conducting an experiment, processing and analyzing the results). The contribution of O.E. Sarmanova (programming and training of neural networks) was supported by the Foundation for the Advancement of Theoretical Physics and Mathematics “BASIS” (Project No. 19-2-6-6-1 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: IR absorption spectra of CDs evaporated from an aqueous suspension.
A) CDs FL spectra under excitation at different wavelengths. B) The excitation-emission matrix of the CDs FL.

Channel-by-channel statistics of the datasets comprising fluorescence spectra of CDs aqueous suspensions in the presence of a single salt. The red line corresponds to the average value for the channel, the black dotted line corresponds to the range of the average intensity change, it is equal to the standard deviation of the intensity value in the channel.

Score plots – EEMs projections into the space of the first three principal components (PCs). The color indicates EEMs corresponding to particular values of the cation concentration. The proximity of the two points (two patterns) location on the score plot indicates their “similarity”.
Results of the trained NNs application to the test dataset. A) MAE of cations’ concentration determination. B) RMSE of cations’ concentration determination.
Characteristics of CDs in an aqueous suspension, concentration 8 mg/L.

head.ce:title: Implementing neural network approach to create carbon-based optical nanosensor of heavy metal ions in liquid media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physics, Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Physics, Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Skobeltsyn Institute of Nuclear Physics, Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Skobeltsyn Institute of Nuclear Physics, Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study is devoted to the creation of multifunctional optical carbon dots-based nanosensor to simultaneously measure concentrations of metal ions (Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+) and NO3 – anion in liquid media. Such nanosensor operates on the basis of its fluorescence (FL) change under the influence of ions in the medium. However, the absence of analytical model, describing CD FL mechanism, the superposition of various luminescence quenching mechanisms during the interaction of carbon dots (CD) with cations, hampers the usage of classical approaches to solve this inverse multiparametric spectroscopic problem. To solve it neural networks were used that analyzed complex fluorescence signal from CD aqueous suspensions comprising Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, NO3 – ions in the concentration range from 0 to 4.95 mM. The following neural network architectures ensured optical spectroscopy inverse problem solution: multilayer perceptrons, 1D and 2D convolutional neural networks.

The developed sensor enables simultaneous determination of the concentrations of heavy metal ions Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+ with a root mean squared error of 0.28 mM, 0.79 mM, 0.24 mM respectively. Based on the data given in the literature we can assert that the accuracy of the studied nanosensor satisfies the needs of monitoring the composition of waste and technological water. The developed nanosensor has a unique multimodality: with the simplicity of the synthesis protocol the sensor enables simultaneous determination of three heavy metal ions concentrations, while analogues are being developed mainly to measure the concentration of one (in rare cases two) heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Mining, metallurgy, production of pesticides and fertilizers, etc. have become global sources of heavy metal pollution, releasing thousands of tons of heavy metal compounds into the environment each year [1]. Heavy metal ions do not biodegrade and accumulate in surface and groundwater, soil, and, ultimately, in living organisms [2] unlike organic contaminants. Heavy metal ions accumulated in an organism exceeding their critical level cause various diseases and disorders [3]. Thus, the control of toxic substances in liquid media is becoming increasingly acute.

Heavy metals ions concentrations in water are measured with amperometry [4], flow-injection analysis [5], chromatography [6], atomic absorption spectroscopy technique [7] and other analytical methods. Optical spectroscopy methods such as fluorescence, absorption, and Raman spectroscopy, are also widely used to determine ions concentration in solutions [8,9]. Though optical methods are less accurate than analytical ones, they can be used to simultaneously determine concentrations of many chemical elements. Moreover, they do not require special preparation of experimental samples, and are non-invasive, fast, and inexpensive.

For determination of the multicomponent medium composition via optical spectroscopy, one requires a special sensor. Its optical properties must be sensitive to each component of the medium. Today, a lot of efforts are channeled into creating fluorescent materials with excellent luminescence, controllable emission wavelength, large Stokes shift, good biocompatibility, and high photo-stability. Significant progress along this path has been achieved through the synthesis of fluorescent metal nanoclusters [10–12], DNA-based luminescent materials [13], etc. Carbon dots (CDs), synthesized for the first time in 2004 [14], have broad prospects for application as optical fluorescent nanosensors in the tasks of media diagnostics [15]. CDs stand out among semiconductor quantum dots and many organic fluorescent dyes for their high photostability, inexpensiveness, and synthesis simplicity, are biocompatible and water-soluble [16,17].

Multiple studies have shown that CD fluorescence (FL) is sensitive to the interaction between CDs and specific ions, which is revealed in luminescence band changing its position and intensity in the presence of certain ions. This fact opens up the possibility of creating a CD-based nanosensor of metal ions. For example, in [18], the authors discovered the effect of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence of CD obtained by hydrothermal method, which allowed to use of CD fluorescence for the Fe3+ ions detection ions and to develop the fast, reliable, and selective method with a detection limit of only 0.72 µM in the dynamic range from 5 to 500 µM. The authors of [19] investigated the possibility of creating an Ag+ and glutathione (GSH) ion sensor based on changes in CD FL intensity. They showed that the CD surface carboxyl groups bind to Ag(I) ions, which causes fluorescence quenching via the static mechanism. The quenched CD fluorescence can be restored by adding GSH due to the high affinity of GSH to Ag(I). The calibration plot of the FL intensity dependence on the Ag(I) concentration is linear in the range of Ag(I) concentrations from 1 to 4 µM, it ensures Ag(I) detection limit as low as 31 nM. The corresponding values for GSH are 5–32 µM and 76 nM, respectively. Finally, the authors determined the concentration of Ag+ cations in the samples of flow and lake waters using CD FL calibration graphs. Thereby they demonstrated the possibility of CDs application as a nanosensor of silver cations. The investigation conducted in [20] showed that high FL sensitivity of the nitrogen-doped CDs to the presence and quantity of Cr4+ ions provided its detection limit of 0.26 µm. In [21], CDs prepared via hydrothermal synthesis and doped with boron, nitrogen, and sulfur atoms were studied. Fluorescence in the red region of such CD showed good selectivity to Fe3+ ions in water. The nanosensor developed by the authors allows detecting Fe3+ ions in the range of their concentration from 0.3 to 546 µM with a detection limit of 90 nM.

Despite the increased interest in the use of CD as optical sensors, currently, there is no consensus in the scientific community on the mechanisms of CD fluorescence formation. This is because CD prepared by hydrothermal method from different precursors under different temperatures and pressures usually contain a variety of structural elements and have different optical characteristics. At the moment, the presence of various surface states, quantum confinement effect, and the formation of molecular complexes (molecular fluorescence) [22] are the most generally recognized mechanisms of CD FL. Thus, the analysis of the fluorescence spectra of CD suspensions in the presence of several ions is hampered by the lack of analytical models reliably describing the CD fluorescent response to changes in the ionic composition of the suspension.

It should be noted that in all studies known to us (including those listed above) CD were considered as unimodal nanosensors, i.e., sensors designed to determine only one parameter. At the same time, in most scientific and applied tasks (for example, in medicine or in various technological processes), one requires simultaneous monitoring of several environmental parameters with high sensitivity and in real-time. It is necessary to monitor multicomponent media, where each of the components can affect the CD luminescence in its own way. Thus, it seems relevant to develop a CD-based multifunctional optical nanosensor to determine several parameters of multicomponent media simultaneously.

Such nanosensor might operate on the basis of its FL spectra change under the influence of various parameters of the medium. However, the solution of such inverse problem is complicated due to several reasons: heterogeneity of the CD surface functional groups, the nonspecific interaction of cations with functional groups, and the superposition of various luminescence quenching mechanisms during the interaction of CD with cations. Together with the absence of analytical model, describing CD FL mechanism, this fact hampers the usage of classical approaches to solve this inverse spectroscopic problem. To overcome the stated difficulties, we suggest using machine learning [23]. Machine learning is being actively introduced into nanosensorics to create optical sensors based on various nanoparticles [24–27]. However, there are no studies describing the use of ML to create a CD-based nanosensor.

In this study, we propose to solve the problem of creating a multifunctional optical CD-based nanosensor of metal ions using machine learning algorithms for analyzing complex CD fluorescence excitation-emission matrices in the presence of various metal ions including heavy metal ions.

Carbon dots were prepared via hydrothermal method. The synthesis framework implies dissolving 1.09 g of citric acid (C6H8O7) in 7 mL of ethylenediamine and adding 20 mL of deionized water. Further the solution was treated in an ultrasonic bath for 5–10 min for homogenous mixing of the initial reagents, then kept for 4 h at a temperature of 190°C in an autoclave. To isolate CDs from synthesis products, one used a track membrane with a pore size of 100 nm and a silica gel with a pore size of 6 nm. The resulting CD suspension was placed in a dialysis bag with a pore size of 1 kDa and stirred in water for 8 h using a magnetic stirrer to get rid of the remnants of precursors and chemical reaction products. The synthesis of CDs was partially performed by our partners at the Northeastern Federal University named after M.K.Ammosov (Yakutsk) [28].

The data from a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Fig. S1a, [28]) shows that the mean diameter of spherical CDs is ∼ 14 nm and the size distribution is consistent with the Gaussian one (Fig. S1b, [28]). The data from atomic force microscope (AFM) of monodisperse CDs (Fig. S2b) shows the average thickness of CDs to be ∼ 1.5 nm. This suggests that these CDs consist of 1–4 graphene layers. The average lateral dimensions of CDs obtained from the SEM data are consistent with the results of the AFM analysis (Fig. S1a, Fig. S2a, [28]).

CDs aqueous suspensions with a nanoparticles concentration of 8 mg/L were prepared. Bi-distilled deionized water (Millipore Milli-Q, conductivity 0.055 μS/cm) was used for the preparation of CDs aqueous suspensions. The Zeta-potentials and particle sizes in aqueous suspensions were determined using the dynamic light scattering method on the Malvern Zetasizer 31 NanoZS device. Table 1 shows the obtained data.
According to the presented data, the studied CDs do not tend to aggregate, as comes from the value of the Zeta-potential module.

The composition of the surface functional groups of synthesized CDs was determined using IR absorption spectroscopy. The IR absorption spectra of CDs evaporated from an aqueous suspension were obtained using a Varian 640-IR FTIR spectrometer equipped with a diamond crystal attachment operating on the principle of total internal reflection disturbance. The registration was carried out in the range of 900–3800 cm−1, the spectral resolution was 4 cm−1. Fig. 1 shows the IR absorption spectra of CDs evaporated from an aqueous suspension.

Fig. 1 shows that the CD’s surface contains functional groups such as CH2, CH3 (1440–1480 cm−1), CO (1580–1630 cm−1, 1650–1690 cm−1), CO (1210–1320 cm−1), CC (1620–1640 cm−1), NH (1630–1660 cm−1, 3380–3420 cm−1, 3480–3540 cm−1), OH (3000–3600 cm−1), CN, and CN (-1300 cm−1).

The aqueous suspensions containing CDs with fixed concentrations of 8 mg/L and Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+ cations with concentrations varying from 0 to 4.95 mM with 0.55 mM increment were prepared. For this purpose Cu(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3 salts were added to the CDs suspension so that Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+ ions were present in suspension in the desired concentration. The ions types and the range of their concentrations were chosen according to the characteristics of real wastewater from metal coating plants [29].

A total of 1000 suspensions were prepared: 27 single-component suspensions (containing one type of cation), 240 double-component suspensions (containing any two types of cations), and 732 triple-component suspensions (containing three types of cations simultaneously). One sample corresponds to the case of a CDs suspension with no ions.

A Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorimeter was used to obtain fluorescence spectra of CDs aqueous suspensions – excitation-emission matrices – in the presence of the studied ions. Signal excitation was carried out by radiation at wavelengths from 250 to 450 nm with 5 nm increment – a total of 41 excitation wavelengths. The fluorescent response of suspensions was recorded in the spectral range from 250 to 750 nm with a step of 1 nm in such way that each CDs suspension fluorescence spectrum at a given excitation wavelength has 500 features. For each suspension, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded under excitation at 41 different wavelengths.

Neural networks (NNs) are a class of biologically inspired machine learning algorithms [29]. In the study, we implemented several neural network approaches that ensure optical spectroscopy inverse problem solution, i.e. determination of heavy metal ions concentrations in liquid media.
Multilayer perceptrons.

Multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) (see Supplementary file, section “Multilayer perceptrons”), also known as fully connected neural networks, have the simplest architecture among neural networks: the output of each neuron of the preceding layer is connected to the input of each neuron of the next layer.

In the present study, we tried several approaches to apply MLPs to the spectral data. Therefore, we formed separate datasets consisting of: (1) fluorescence spectra, obtained under excitation with radiation at one wavelength; (2) three fluorescence spectra, obtained under excitation with radiation at three wavelengths; (3) 41 fluorescence spectra, obtained under excitation with radiation at 41 wavelengths. To sum up, MLPs were applied to the following data:
1.
MLP_1W: Fluorescence spectra of CDs suspensions under excitation at 350 nm wavelength. According to the obtained data (Fig. 2 A), the absolute fluorescence intensity of CD aqueous suspensions is maximal when excited by radiation with a wavelength of 350 nm. This ensures maximum dispersion in spectroscopic data when the ion concentration changes. As each CDs suspension fluorescence spectrum at a given excitation wavelength has 500 features (see section 2.3.), so the training patterns represent vectors of dimension [1x500] (see S.5).
2.
MLP_3W: Fluorescence spectra of CDs suspensions under excitation at three wavelengths (250, 350, and 450 nm). The chosen three wavelengths correspond to the boundaries and the middle of the range used in experiment. For each pattern these three fluorescence spectra were concatenated to form a single vector of dimension [1x1500] (see S.6).
3.
MLP_41W: Fluorescence spectra of CDs suspensions under excitation at 41 wavelengths. Excitation-emission matrices (EEMs) were stretched into vectors of dimension [1x20500], similarly, how it was done for three excitation wavelengths.

Applying multilayer perceptrons to the spectra under fluorescence excitation at one particular wavelength (MLP_1W) seems to be the simplest approach to the problem of diagnostics of multicomponent medium. The method requires the least computational resources (perceptrons are one of the simplest models; besides, the number of input features, in this particular case, is small) and experimental resources (one has to register the fluorescent signal only at one excitation wavelength).

The use of three excitation wavelengths is a compromise option. On the one hand, the use of several wavelengths enables increasing information about the system, and on the other hand, the use of only three wavelengths out of 41 significantly facilitates the experiment and reduces the time spent on obtaining spectra.
Convolutional neural networks.

Convolutional neural networks (CNN) are a special kind of neural networks for processing data with ordered correlated features (e.g. images) [30] (see Supplementary file, section “Convolutional neural networks”).

Due to the 1-D nature of spectroscopic data (except for fluorescence spectroscopy with registration of excitation-emission matrices) and due to the high degree of data correlation between adjacent spectral channels, 1D convolutional neural networks (1D CNN) seem to be appropriate to solve inverse spectroscopic problems. Researchers apply 1D CNN to solve inverse problems of optical spectroscopy for practical purposes, e.g. to distinguish aristolochic acids with infrared spectroscopy [31], to estimate various soil properties with visible and near-infrared diffuse reflectance spectroscopy [32], to predict the glucose concentration of blood with Raman spectroscopy [33], etc. 2D convolutional networks are applied to spectroscopic data much less frequently because, as a rule, spectroscopists use a single wavelength of signal (Raman scattering / optical absorption) excitation, and the data have one-dimensional nature. However, in the case of fluorescence spectroscopy with registration of excitation-emission matrices (2D arrays), it is possible to apply 2D CNN to process the data [34].

In the present study-two approaches were implemented to apply CNN to spectroscopic data:
1.
1D convolutional networks (S.7). In this case the pattern is a tensor [41x1x500], where the number of channels stands in the first place, and then the spatial dimensions of the “image” (CNN_1D) are in the second and third dimensions;
2.
2D convolutional networks (S.8). In this case the pattern is a tensor [1x41x500], where the number of channels stands in the first place, and then the spatial dimensions of the “image” (CNN_2D). Unlike MLP, CNNs 'consider' the relative location of features (spectral channels). This property enables extracting additional information from the data since the values of fluorescence intensity in adjacent spectral channels are highly correlated. Moreover, 2D CNNs 'consider' the mutual arrangement of channels in both the excitation and fluorescence spectra, while 1D CNNs - only in the fluorescence spectra.
Metrics.

We used mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE) to evaluate the quality of the trained networks:
(1)
MAE =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
y i
-
y i
^
RMSE =
∑
i = 1
N
(
y i
-
y i
^
)
2
N

where N is the number of patterns,
y i
is the real value of the target variable,
y i
^
is the prediction of the model.

Neural networks belong to the class of data-driven methods, so for their successful application it is necessary to have representative datasets. Thus, 1000 excitation-emission matrices (see S.4.) of CDs aqueous suspensions in the presence of one, two, and three types of cations (Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+) and NO3 – anion with known concentrations were obtained experimentally. The task was to determine the concentration of each of the four ions (Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, NO3 –) from the CDs FL suspensions spectra.

To prevent NNs overfitting (a situation, when NN memorizes training patterns but does not generalize data patterns) and to evaluate their performance, the original dataset was randomly divided into training, validation and test sets in a ratio of 70:20:10, respectively. The training set was used directly to train the models; the validation set - to ensure timely stopping of training (training was stopped if the mean squared error on the validation set did not decrease during 100 epochs); the test set - to check the quality of trained models on independent data. The sets did not overlap.

Fivefold cross-validation prevents the way of splitting the data into sets from influencing the results. For that reason, the initial dataset was split into train/validation/test sets 5 times. The results of using neural networks with five realizations of splitting into sets were averaged. Additionally, to exclude the influence of the weights' random initialization, NNs were trained 3 times followed by averaging the results within each cross-validation fold. Thus, the quality of the inverse problem solution by a network of a certain configuration was evaluated on the basis of the results of 15 neural networks of this configuration.

The CDs FL spectra represent a wide band with a maximum in the region of 442 nm (Fig. 2. (A)). CDs luminescence is most intensively excited by radiation with a wavelength of 350 nm. This information will be used when implementing neural network approaches to solve the inverse problem of fluorescence spectroscopy.

The study of the CDs' photoluminescence stability in the presence of the Cu2+, Ni2+, and Cr3+ ions showed that during the observation time (10 days) there was no significant change in CDs' photoluminescence (Fig.S9). The figure shows the photoluminescence spectra of CD excited at a wavelength of 350 nm. The deviation of the integral intensity from the average value is ∼ 1 %.

Fig. 3 shows the channel-by-channel statistics of three datasets comprising fluorescence spectra of CDs aqueous suspensions in the presence of a single salt. As the ranges of cations concentration changes are the same for all cations, fluorescence of the studied CDs is least sensitive to Ni2+ concentration changes in the suspension. This suggests that the error in determining the concentration of Ni2+ using CDs FL spectra will be maximal.

In this study, we performed preliminary data analysis just to investigate the data structure and identify the factors that affect the responses of models most firmly. The data projected into the space of the principal components (PCs) were not used for further training of the models. The principal component analysis allows one to compress data from a high-dimensional space into a low-dimensional space, shifting from the space of initial features into the space of their linear combinations. Fig. 4 shows score plots - projections of patterns into the space of the first three PCs. The color indicates patterns corresponding to particular values of the target parameter (cation concentration). The proximity of the two points (two patterns) location on the score plot indicates their “similarity”. In cases of Cu2+ and Cr3+ cations, one can highlight clear clusters on the score plots, formed by the patterns with similar concentrations of the corresponding ions. The situation is different for the Ni2+ cation - it is impossible to isolate clusters, which suggests that the fluorescence spectra of suspensions with similar or equal nickel concentrations may differ greatly. This is due to the lower sensitivity of CDs fluorescence to Ni2+ ions, which confirms the conclusion made at the previous step: the error in determining the concentration of Ni2+ using CDs will be maximal.

Several configurations of NNs are used in this study to find a balance between the quality of solving the problem, the computing power required to solve the problem, and the labor costs associated with the amount of experimentally obtained data.

The results of training MLP models are somewhat expected. The higher the number of fluorescence spectra representing excitation at different wavelengths fed to the model within one training pattern, the better the quality of the problem solution became.

The MLP_41W, CNN_1D, and CNN_2D models reveal an interesting sequence when all obtained data is considered. Remember that MLP_41W considers all input features independently – their mutual location is inconsiderable for the network. CNN_1D treats the relative location of channels only in the 1D fluorescence spectra of the suspension, and CNN_2D - both in the fluorescence spectra (“image width”) and in the excitation spectra (“image height”). Thus, in the series MLP_41W, CNN_1D, CNN_2D, the “amount of information” considered by the model increases, which affects the quality of the problem solution. Thus, 2D CNNs enabled achieving a minimum RMSE, as they ‘consider’ additional information about the mutual arrangement of spectral channels, both in the fluorescence and excitation spectra.

The use of MAE as a metric is based on the assumption that all the individual differences are weighted equally in the average, while RMSE gives a relatively high weight to large errors. Fig. 5 shows MAE and RMSE for ions’ concentrations determination. Serious differences in MAE and RMSE values (here, RMSE is on average 24 % larger than MAE) indicate variance in the individual errors over the patterns. Thus, the study of the distribution of the errors among specific patterns may give the key to understanding how to improve the quality of the problem solution.

Limit of detection (LOD) is a determinant parameter for a photonic biosensor indicating the lowest concentrations that can be reliably detected [35]. In our case the evaluation of the sensor’s LOD deserves a separate discussion. In the study we work with fluorescence excitation-emission matrices, meaning that NNs simultaneously analyze fluorescence spectra excited with radiation in the range from 250 to 450 nm with 5 nm increment. Consequently, the inverse problem is solved considering the information in the “space” of both the emission spectra of fluorescence and the spectra of its excitation. The results of solving the inverse problem indicate that in the concentration range from 0 to 4.95 mM, neural networks can determine the concentration of an ion with RMSE of 0.28 mM, 0.79 mM, 0.24 mM for Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, respectively. It follows from this, that the NN will not be able to distinguish between changes in ion concentration in the ranges from 0 mM to the above RMSE values. Thus, we consider the obtained RMSE to be the LOD of the studied sensor.
Based on the data given in the paper [29], one can assert that the accuracy of the studied nanosensor satisfies the needs of monitoring the composition of waste and technological water.

This study presents a unique approach to create simple and cheap optical CDs-based nanosensor capable of simultaneous measuring of four parameters (Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, NO3 – concentrations) of a liquid medium using machine learning approach.

It was found that the fluorescence of CDs prepared via hydrothermal synthesis route is extremely sensitive to the changes in Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, NO3 – ions concentrations. This enabled using CDs to develop a fluorescent nanosensor for identifying heavy metal cations, NO3 – anion and determining the concentration of each of them in multicomponent liquid media. To solve this multiparametric inverse problem, 1000 CDs FL excitation-emission matrices in suspensions were used and five NNs architectures were applied.

2D CNNs enabled achieving a minimum RMSE of cations concentration determination compared to the multilayer perceptrons and 1D CNNs, as 2D CNN captures dependencies of the data in two dimensions: they ‘consider’ additional information about the mutual arrangement of spectral channels, both in the fluorescence and excitation spectra. Thus, the joint consideration of the excitation and emission spectra of CD FL using a neural network approach for the first time allowed to perform simultaneous determination of the concentration of 3 heavy metal ions and NO3 – anion using an optical nanosensor.

The results of solving the inverse problem indicate that in the concentration range from 0 to 4.95 mM, neural networks can determine the concentration of an ion with RMSE of 0.28 mM, 0.79 mM, 0.24 mM for Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, respectively. For example, in [29], wastewater from three metal coating plants was studied, and it was shown that the content of Cu2+, Ni2+ and Cr3+ ions varied in the ranges 0.7–5.3 mM, 6.7–9.0 mM, 0.8–3.7 mM, respectively. Thus, the accuracy of determining the concentrations of these ions obtained in our work using carbon nanosensor indicates its potential for controlling waste and technological waters composition. With the simplicity of the synthesis protocol the sensor enables simultaneous determination of three heavy metal ions concentrations, while analogues are being developed mainly to measure the concentration of one (in rare cases two) heavy metal ions [36].

The study was carried out at the expense of the Russian Science Foundation No. 22-12-00138, https://rscf.ru/project /22-12-00138 / (T.A.Dolenko, S.A.Burikov, K.A.Laptinskiy – planning and conducting an experiment, processing and analyzing the results). The contribution of O.E. Sarmanova (programming and training of neural networks) was supported by theFoundation for the Advancement of Theoretical Physics and Mathematics “BASIS” (Project No. 19-2-6-6-1).

O.E. Sarmanova: Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Funding acquisition. K.A. Laptinskiy: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. S.A. Burikov: Methodology, Investigation. G.N. Chugreeva: Formal analysis, Investigation. T.A. Dolenko: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors of the study express their deep gratitude to A.E. Tomskaya for the synthesis of carbon dots. Some of the experimental results used in this work were obtained using a FTIR spectrometer purchased under the Development Program of Moscow State University (Agreement No. 65, 04.10.2021).
Data and code are available on request.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
